news_summary: Breakthroughs in superconductivity, energy-efficient chip technology, digital avatars enabling performances for those with MND, and innovative quantum batteries demonstrate remarkable advances in energy, computing, and human connection.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées dans le domaine de la supraconductivité, les puces à faible consommation d'énergie, les avatars numériques permettant aux personnes atteintes de SLA de s'exprimer, ainsi que les batteries quantiques innovantes témoignent des progrès remarquables réalisés dans les domaines de l'énergie, de l'informatique et des relations humaines.
news_summary_es: Los avances en superconductividad, la tecnología de chips de bajo consumo, los avatares digitales que permiten actuar a las personas con ELA y las innovadoras baterías cuánticas ponen de manifiesto notables progresos en los ámbitos de la energía, la informática y las relaciones humanas.

articles.title: This superconductivity dies then comes back to life
articles.summary: A strange new kind of superconductivity has been uncovered in uranium ditelluride (UTe2), where electricity flows with zero resistance—but only under extremely strong magnetic fields that should normally destroy it. Even more surprising, the superconductivity disappears at first and then dramatically reappears at even higher fields, earning it the nickname the “Lazarus phase.”
articles.content:
Researchers have uncovered and explained an unusual form of superconductivity that only appears under extremely strong magnetic fields. The work, led in part by Rice University physicist Andriy Nevidomskyy, was published in Science and describes how uranium ditelluride (UTe 2 ) forms a distinctive superconducting halo when exposed to intense magnetic conditions.

Under normal circumstances, magnetic fields disrupt superconductors. Even relatively modest fields tend to weaken superconductivity, while stronger ones usually eliminate it entirely once a critical limit is reached. UTe 2 breaks this rule. In 2019, scientists discovered that it can remain superconducting in magnetic fields hundreds of times stronger than what typical materials can withstand.

"When I first saw the experimental data, I was stunned," said Nevidomskyy, a member of the Rice Advanced Materials Institute and the Rice Center for Quantum Materials. "The superconductivity was first suppressed by the magnetic field as expected but then reemerged in higher fields and only for what appeared to be a narrow field direction. There was no immediate explanation for this puzzling behavior."

A Superconducting "Resurrection" at Extreme Fields

This strange behavior, first observed by teams at the University of Maryland (UMD) and the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST), quickly drew attention across the physics community. In UTe 2 , superconductivity disappears below 10 Tesla, which is already an extremely strong field, but unexpectedly returns at field strengths above 40 Tesla.

Scientists have named this revival the Lazarus phase. It turns out that this phase depends strongly on the angle between the magnetic field and the material's crystal structure.

Working with collaborators at UMD and NIST, Nevidomskyy helped map how this high-field superconductivity changes with direction. Their measurements showed that the superconducting region forms a toroidal, or doughnutlike, shape that surrounds a particular axis within the crystal.

"Our measurements revealed a three-dimensional superconducting halo that wraps around the hard b-axis of the crystal," said Sylvia Lewin of NIST, a co-lead author on the study. "This was a surprising and beautiful result."

Building a Model to Explain the Halo

To understand what was happening, Nevidomskyy created a theoretical model that could explain the observations without depending heavily on uncertain microscopic details. The model uses a phenomenological approach, focusing on the overall behavior rather than the exact underlying mechanisms that cause electrons to pair into Cooper pairs.

The results matched the experimental data closely, especially the unusual way superconductivity changes with the direction of the magnetic field. The model shows how orientation plays a crucial role in whether superconductivity survives or returns in UTe 2 .

How Magnetism and Superconductivity Interact

The study also revealed that Cooper pairs in this material behave as if they carry angular momentum, similar to a spinning object. When a magnetic field is applied, it interacts with this motion, creating a directional effect that produces the observed halo pattern.

This insight helps explain how magnetism and superconductivity can coexist in materials with strong directional properties like UT e2 .

"One of the experimental observations is the sudden increase in the sample magnetization, what we call a metamagnetic transition," said NIST's Peter Czajka, co-lead author on the study. "The high-field superconductivity only appears once the field magnitude has reached this value, itself highly angle-dependent."

Scientists are still debating what causes this metamagnetic transition and how it influences superconductivity. Nevidomskyy said the new model could help clarify this open question.

"While the nature of the pairing glue in this material remains to be understood, knowing that the Cooper pairs carry a magnetic moment is a key outcome of this study and should help guide future investigations," he said.

Research Team and Support

The study involved Corey Frank and Nicholas Butch from NIST; Hyeok Yoon, Yun Suk Eo, Johnpierre Paglione and Gicela Saucedo Salas from UMD; and G. Timothy Noe and John Singleton from Los Alamos National Laboratory. Funding was provided by the U.S. Department of Energy and the National Science Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in superconductivity, revealing a new phenomenon where superconductivity reemerges under extremely strong magnetic fields, defying conventional understanding. This discovery has broad implications for physics and materials science, potentially impacting future technologies reliant on superconductors. The article provides detailed context about the discovery, experimental observations, theoretical modeling, and collaborative research, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful real-world impact and substance.
articles.personality_title: Scientists discover ‘Lazarus phase’ where superconductivity returns under strong magnets
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Superconductors are materials that let electricity flow without any resistance. Usually, strong magnetic fields stop this special state from working. But uranium ditelluride (UTe2) acts differently.

**What happened** – Researchers found that in UTe2, superconductivity disappears at a strong magnetic field but then comes back at even stronger fields. This surprising return happens only when the magnetic field is pointed in certain directions. Scientists call this the “Lazarus phase” because the superconductivity seems to come back to life.

**Impact** – This discovery challenges what scientists thought about superconductors and magnets. It shows that superconductivity can survive and even reappear under extreme conditions. The team also created a model explaining how the direction of the magnetic field creates a doughnut-shaped superconducting area around part of the crystal. This helps us understand how magnetism and superconductivity can work together in new ways.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will study why this “resurrection” happens and how the magnetic properties of the material influence it. Understanding this could lead to new materials or technologies that use superconductors in stronger magnetic environments.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists uncovered a rare “Lazarus phase” where superconductivity in uranium ditelluride disappears and then returns under very strong magnetic fields, opening new paths for future research.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques découvrent la « phase Lazare » où la supraconductivité revient sous de forts champs magnétiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les supraconducteurs sont des matériaux qui laissent passer l’électricité sans aucune résistance. Habituellement, les champs magnétiques forts arrêtent cet état spécial. Mais le ditellurure d’uranium (UTe2) se comporte différemment.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont découvert que dans l’UTe2, la supraconductivité disparaît sous un champ magnétique fort, puis réapparaît sous un champ encore plus fort. Ce retour surprenant ne se produit que lorsque le champ magnétique est dirigé dans certaines directions. Les scientifiques appellent cela la « phase Lazare » car la supraconductivité semble renaître.

**Impact** – Cette découverte remet en question ce que les scientifiques pensaient des supraconducteurs et des aimants. Elle montre que la supraconductivité peut survivre et même réapparaître dans des conditions extrêmes. L’équipe a aussi créé un modèle expliquant comment la direction du champ magnétique forme une zone supraconductrice en forme d’anneau autour d’une partie du cristal. Cela aide à comprendre comment le magnétisme et la supraconductivité peuvent fonctionner ensemble de nouvelles façons.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont étudier pourquoi cette « résurrection » a lieu et comment les propriétés magnétiques du matériau l’influencent. Comprendre cela pourrait mener à de nouveaux matériaux ou technologies utilisant des supraconducteurs dans des environnements magnétiques plus forts.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont découvert une rare « phase Lazare » où la supraconductivité dans le ditellurure d’uranium disparaît puis revient sous de très forts champs magnétiques, ouvrant de nouvelles voies de recherche.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos descubren la « fase Lázaro » donde la superconductividad regresa bajo campos magnéticos fuertes
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los superconductores son materiales que permiten que la electricidad fluya sin resistencia alguna. Normalmente, los campos magnéticos fuertes detienen este estado especial. Pero el ditelururo de uranio (UTe2) actúa de forma diferente.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores encontraron que en UTe2, la superconductividad desaparece con un campo magnético fuerte pero luego regresa con campos aún más fuertes. Este sorprendente regreso ocurre solo cuando el campo magnético apunta en ciertas direcciones. Los científicos llaman a esto la «fase Lázaro» porque la superconductividad parece volver a la vida.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento desafía lo que los científicos pensaban sobre superconductores y magnetismo. Muestra que la superconductividad puede sobrevivir y reaparecer en condiciones extremas. El equipo también creó un modelo que explica cómo la dirección del campo magnético crea un área superconductora en forma de dona alrededor de una parte del cristal. Esto ayuda a entender cómo el magnetismo y la superconductividad pueden trabajar juntos de nuevas maneras.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores estudiarán por qué ocurre esta «resurrección» y cómo las propiedades magnéticas del material la afectan. Comprender esto podría llevar a nuevos materiales o tecnologías que usen superconductores en ambientes magnéticos más fuertes.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos descubrieron una rara «fase Lázaro» donde la superconductividad en el ditelururo de uranio desaparece y luego regresa bajo campos magnéticos muy fuertes, abriendo nuevas vías para la investigación.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, translucent toroidal halo of light encircling a stylized crystalline structure symbolizing uranium ditelluride, set against a softly shaded background that subtly suggests intense magnetic fields through swirling, directional lines, capturing the mysterious disappearance and reemergence of superconductivity without depicting any human figures.

articles.title: This new chip could slash data center energy waste
articles.summary: A new chip design from UC San Diego could make data centers far more energy-efficient by rethinking how power is converted for GPUs. By combining vibrating piezoelectric components with a clever circuit layout, the system overcomes limitations of traditional designs. The prototype achieved impressive efficiency and delivered much more power than previous attempts. Though not ready for widespread use yet, it points to a promising future for high-performance computing.
articles.content:
As data centers consume more energy to support growing digital demands, engineers at the University of California San Diego have introduced a new chip design that could make powering graphics processing units (GPUs) more efficient. The innovation focuses on a key function in electronics: converting high voltages into the lower levels required by computing hardware. In laboratory testing, a prototype chip successfully performed this type of voltage conversion with high efficiency under conditions similar to those found in modern data centers.

The findings, published in Nature Communications, suggest the potential for smaller and more energy-efficient systems in advanced computing environments.

Rethinking DC-DC Converters for Modern Electronics

At the center of the new design is an improved version of a widely used component known as a DC-DC step-down converter. These converters are found in nearly all electronic devices and serve as a critical link between power sources and sensitive circuits. Their job is to take a high incoming voltage and reduce it to the exact level needed for safe operation.

In data centers, electricity is often distributed at 48 volts, while GPU processors typically require much lower voltages, usually between 1 and 5 volts. Efficiently managing this large voltage drop has become increasingly challenging as computing systems grow more powerful and compact.

Limits of Traditional Power Conversion Technology

Conventional step-down converters often struggle when dealing with large differences between input and output voltage. As that gap increases, efficiency drops and it becomes harder to supply enough current. Most existing designs rely on magnetic components such as inductors. While these components have been refined over many years, they are approaching their practical limits and are becoming harder to improve further.

"We've gotten so good at designing inductive converters that there's not really much room left to improve them to meet future needs," said study senior author Patrick Mercier, professor in the Department of Electrical and Computer Engineering at the UC San Diego Jacobs School of Engineering.

Exploring Piezoelectric Resonators as an Alternative

To move beyond these limitations, Mercier and his team, including first author Jae-Young Ko, an electrical and computer engineering Ph.D. student at UC San Diego, investigated a different approach using piezoelectric resonators. These small devices store and transfer energy through mechanical vibrations rather than magnetic fields.

Converters based on piezoelectric components could offer several advantages. They have the potential to be smaller, more energy dense, more efficient, and easier to manufacture at scale. "They have a lot of room to grow and have the potential to deliver better performance than anything that's come before them," Mercier said.

However, earlier versions of piezoelectric converters have had difficulty maintaining efficiency and delivering sufficient power when handling large voltage differences.

Hybrid Design Achieves High Efficiency and Power Output

To overcome these issues, the researchers created a hybrid converter that combines a piezoelectric resonator with small, commercially available capacitors arranged in a carefully designed configuration. This setup enables the system to handle larger voltage conversions more effectively.

The team incorporated this design into a prototype chip and tested its performance. The device successfully converted 48 volts down to 4.8 volts -- a level commonly required in data centers -- with a peak efficiency of 96.2 percent. It also delivered roughly four times more output current than previous piezoelectric-based designs.

This hybrid approach offers several benefits. It creates multiple pathways for energy to move through the system, reduces wasted power, and lessens the strain on the resonator. Together, these improvements enhance both efficiency and power delivery while only slightly increasing the chip's size.

Challenges and Next Steps for Real-World Use

Although the technology shows strong promise, it is still in the early stages of development. The researchers view it as an important step toward overcoming the constraints of current power conversion systems. Future efforts will focus on refining materials, improving circuit designs, and developing better packaging methods.

One challenge is that piezoelectric resonators physically vibrate, which means they cannot be attached to circuit boards using standard soldering techniques. New integration strategies will be needed to incorporate them into electronic systems, Mercier explained.

"Piezoelectric-based converters aren't quite ready to replace existing power converter technologies yet," Mercier added. "But they offer a trajectory for improvement. We need to continue to improve on multiple areas -- materials, circuits and packaging -- to make this technology ready for data center applications."

This project was supported in part by the Power Management Integration Center (PMIC), an Industry-University Cooperative Research Center (IUCRC) funded by the National Science Foundation (award number 2052809).
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in energy-efficient chip design for data centers, which could substantially reduce energy waste on a large scale. The innovation addresses a critical challenge in power conversion for GPUs, with demonstrated high efficiency and power output in prototype testing. Although still in development, the research offers meaningful potential benefits for the broader computing industry and environmental impact by lowering energy consumption in data centers.
articles.personality_title: New chip design promises major energy savings for data centers
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Data centers use a lot of electricity to run the computers that keep the internet and apps working. One big challenge is safely lowering the high voltage from power lines to the lower voltage that computer parts like GPUs need.

**What happened** – Engineers at the University of California San Diego created a new chip that uses tiny vibrating parts called piezoelectric resonators combined with special circuits. This chip efficiently lowers voltage from 48 volts to about 4.8 volts, which is common for GPUs, reaching over 96% efficiency in tests.

**Impact** – This new design is different from traditional converters that rely on magnetic parts and have reached their limits. The piezoelectric chip can deliver four times more power than earlier similar attempts and wastes less energy, making data centers more energy-efficient.

**What's next step** – Researchers will work on improving the materials, circuit design, and ways to connect these vibrating parts to circuit boards, since they can’t be attached using usual methods. This will help move the technology closer to real-world use in data centers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new chip using vibrating piezoelectric parts could make powering data center GPUs much more energy-efficient, reducing electricity waste in the future.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle puce promet d'importantes économies d'énergie pour les centres de données
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les centres de données consomment beaucoup d’électricité pour faire fonctionner les ordinateurs qui soutiennent Internet et les applications. Un grand défi est d’abaisser en toute sécurité la haute tension des lignes électriques à la tension plus basse nécessaire aux composants comme les GPU.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des ingénieurs de l’Université de Californie à San Diego ont créé une nouvelle puce qui utilise de petites pièces vibrantes appelées résonateurs piézoélectriques combinées à des circuits spéciaux. Cette puce réduit efficacement la tension de 48 volts à environ 4,8 volts, ce qui est courant pour les GPU, avec plus de 96 % d’efficacité lors des tests.

**Impact** – Ce nouveau design diffère des convertisseurs traditionnels qui utilisent des composants magnétiques et ont atteint leurs limites. La puce piézoélectrique peut fournir quatre fois plus de puissance que les tentatives précédentes similaires et gaspille moins d’énergie, rendant les centres de données plus efficaces.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont améliorer les matériaux, la conception des circuits et les méthodes pour connecter ces pièces vibrantes aux cartes électroniques, car elles ne peuvent pas être fixées de manière habituelle. Cela aidera à rapprocher la technologie d’une utilisation réelle.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Une nouvelle puce utilisant des pièces vibrantes piézoélectriques pourrait rendre l’alimentation des GPU dans les centres de données beaucoup plus efficace, réduisant ainsi le gaspillage d’électricité à l’avenir.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo diseño de chip promete grandes ahorros de energía para centros de datos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los centros de datos usan mucha electricidad para que funcionen las computadoras que mantienen Internet y las aplicaciones. Un gran desafío es bajar de forma segura el alto voltaje de las líneas eléctricas al voltaje más bajo que necesitan partes como las GPU.

**Qué pasó** – Ingenieros de la Universidad de California en San Diego crearon un nuevo chip que usa pequeñas piezas vibrantes llamadas resonadores piezoeléctricos combinadas con circuitos especiales. Este chip reduce eficientemente el voltaje de 48 voltios a unos 4.8 voltios, común para las GPU, alcanzando más del 96 % de eficiencia en pruebas.

**Impacto** – Este diseño es diferente a los convertidores tradicionales que usan partes magnéticas y que han llegado a sus límites. El chip piezoeléctrico puede entregar cuatro veces más potencia que intentos similares anteriores y desperdicia menos energía, haciendo los centros de datos más eficientes.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores mejorarán los materiales, el diseño del circuito y las formas de conectar estas partes vibrantes a las placas de circuitos, ya que no pueden fijarse con métodos comunes. Esto ayudará a acercar la tecnología a su uso real.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo chip que usa piezas vibrantes piezoeléctricas podría hacer que alimentar las GPU en centros de datos sea mucho más eficiente, reduciendo el desperdicio de electricidad en el futuro.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned drawing of a glowing, intricately patterned microchip at the center, surrounded by softly vibrating crystalline structures symbolizing piezoelectric resonators, with gentle arcs of flowing electrical energy smoothly stepping down from a bright 48-volt source on one side to a smaller, calm 4.8-volt output on the other, all rendered in natural, muted colors to evoke innovation and energy efficiency in advanced computing.

articles.title: Dancer with MND performs on stage again through digital avatar
articles.summary: Breanna Olson said brainwave tech was able to re-establish the expression and connection she felt had gone.
articles.content:
Dentsu

Breanna hopes her experience will help people with disabilities be viewed "less as sick people or that something is wrong with us, but more like we have value and talents and wisdom"
articles.reasoning: The article highlights a meaningful use of brainwave technology to enable a dancer with motor neuron disease (MND) to perform again through a digital avatar. This represents a significant positive impact by restoring expression and connection for people with disabilities, promoting their value and talents. The story focuses on a single inspiring technological breakthrough with broad societal implications for disability inclusion.
articles.personality_title: Dancer with motor neuron disease performs again using brainwave technology
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Breanna Olson is a dancer who has motor neuron disease (MND), a condition that makes it hard to move and express herself physically. This disease usually stops people from performing activities they love.

**What happened** – Using special brainwave technology, Breanna was able to perform on stage again through a digital avatar. This avatar moves and expresses what Breanna thinks and feels, even though her body cannot. The performance took place recently and showed how technology can help people with disabilities connect and share their talents.

**Impact** – This is important because it gives people like Breanna a new way to communicate and express themselves. Instead of being seen only as sick or limited, they can show their skills and wisdom. The technology helped Breanna feel connected and able to perform, which she thought she had lost.

**What's next step** – Breanna hopes her experience will encourage more development of such technologies and change how society views people with disabilities. This could lead to more opportunities for people to use technology to share their talents and be included in many areas of life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Brainwave technology allowed a dancer with motor neuron disease to perform again through a digital avatar, opening new ways for people with disabilities to express themselves.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une danseuse atteinte de sclérose latérale amyotrophique performe à nouveau grâce à la technologie des ondes cérébrales
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Breanna Olson est une danseuse atteinte de sclérose latérale amyotrophique (SLA), une maladie qui rend difficile le mouvement et l'expression physique. Cette maladie empêche généralement les personnes d'exercer leurs passions.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Grâce à une technologie spéciale des ondes cérébrales, Breanna a pu de nouveau performer sur scène via un avatar numérique. Cet avatar bouge et exprime ce que Breanna pense et ressent, même si son corps ne peut plus. La performance a eu lieu récemment et montre comment la technologie peut aider les personnes en situation de handicap à se connecter et à partager leurs talents.

**Impact** – C’est important car cela offre aux personnes comme Breanna un nouveau moyen de communiquer et de s’exprimer. Plutôt que d’être vues seulement comme malades ou limitées, elles peuvent montrer leurs compétences et leur sagesse. La technologie a aidé Breanna à se sentir connectée et capable de performer, ce qu’elle pensait avoir perdu.

**Prochaine étape** – Breanna espère que son expérience encouragera le développement de telles technologies et changera la façon dont la société perçoit les personnes handicapées. Cela pourrait ouvrir plus d’opportunités pour utiliser la technologie afin de partager leurs talents et d’être inclus dans de nombreux domaines.

**En une phrase** – La technologie des ondes cérébrales a permis à une danseuse atteinte de SLA de performer à nouveau grâce à un avatar numérique, ouvrant de nouvelles façons pour les personnes handicapées de s’exprimer.
articles.personality_title_es: Bailarina con ELA vuelve a actuar gracias a tecnología de ondas cerebrales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Breanna Olson es una bailarina que tiene esclerosis lateral amiotrófica (ELA), una enfermedad que dificulta el movimiento y la expresión física. Esta enfermedad suele impedir que las personas hagan las actividades que aman.

**Qué pasó** – Usando tecnología especial de ondas cerebrales, Breanna pudo actuar nuevamente en el escenario a través de un avatar digital. Este avatar se mueve y expresa lo que Breanna piensa y siente, aunque su cuerpo no pueda. La actuación fue reciente y mostró cómo la tecnología puede ayudar a las personas con discapacidades a conectarse y compartir sus talentos.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque da a personas como Breanna una nueva forma de comunicarse y expresarse. En vez de ser vistas solo como enfermas o limitadas, pueden mostrar sus habilidades y sabiduría. La tecnología ayudó a Breanna a sentirse conectada y capaz de actuar, algo que pensaba que había perdido.

**Próximo paso** – Breanna espera que su experiencia anime a desarrollar más estas tecnologías y cambie cómo la sociedad ve a las personas con discapacidades. Esto podría llevar a más oportunidades para que usen la tecnología para compartir sus talentos y ser incluidos en muchos aspectos de la vida.

**Una frase clave** – La tecnología de ondas cerebrales permitió a una bailarina con ELA actuar nuevamente a través de un avatar digital, abriendo nuevas formas para que las personas con discapacidades se expresen.
articles.image_prompt: A graceful, translucent digital avatar of a dancer composed of flowing light and soft geometric shapes, performing on a softly glowing stage surrounded by warm, gentle hues, with subtle abstract representations of connection and empowerment radiating outward.

articles.title: Quantum batteries could be charged by reversing time
articles.summary: Physicists have shown how time can effectively be reversed for some quantum systems, which would allow for new ways to harvest energy
articles.content:
Physicists have shown how time can effectively be reversed for some quantum systems, which would allow for new ways to harvest energy

Quantum batteries could harvest energy by reversing time’s arrow da-kuk/Getty Images

A method that can reverse the flow of time in quantum systems could one day be used to help charge quantum batteries.

For every process we observe in the universe, events appear to happen in only one direction, following an apparent arrow of time. But most physical laws and equations work whether time flows forwards or backwards.

Physicists have different explanations for why a discrepancy exists between the observed forward arrow of time and the permitted two-way flow. For example, the second law of thermodynamics says that systems are more likely to become disordered as time goes on, creating a preferred direction of time.

In the quantum world, the arrow of time is defined differently. Quantum processes, like classical physical laws, can run in either direction, but we can define an arrow of time by comparing our measurements of a quantum system with our calculations of how a quantum system should change over time. When these line up with a particular statistical pattern, we can say the system is moving forwards in time.

Now, Luis Pedro García-Pintos at Los Alamos National Laboratory in New Mexico and his colleagues have found a way to mimic this statistical signature by reverse-engineering the changes that measurements make to a quantum system and effectively undoing them, making it appear to an observer as if a quantum system were running backwards in time.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Lost in Space-Time Untangle mind-bending physics, maths and the weirdness of reality with our monthly, special-guest-written newsletter. Sign up to newsletter

“We apply fields and control tools on the system that can undo what is happening due to the measurements,” says García-Pintos. “If the measurement was going to push my system up, I can make it go back down. Because we’re able to counteract the effective measurements, we can produce trajectories that are more consistent with the process having been backwards than forward.”

For example, the team suggests you could manipulate the arrow of time in a qubit, the computational component of a quantum computer, by measuring one of its properties, such as its spin, but in an indirect way to avoid upsetting the qubit’s delicate quantum state, allowing it to be constantly measured as it changes over time. This signal can then be used to calculate how to change what the arrow of time looks like by applying a pulse of microwave radiation.

The technique could also allow energy to be harvested from quantum systems where you have to make measurements, says García-Pintos, which could one day be useful for applications like quantum batteries or miniature quantum engines. This is because whenever a measurement is made on a quantum system, it injects energy into that system.

But the careful adjustments made to mimic the changing quantum arrow of time can effectively redirect this energy, harvesting it for other uses. “As a result, you’re getting energy out of it,” says García-Pintos. “You have a mechanism where you’re using measurements as a thermodynamic resource.”

It is a clever idea, says Mauro Paternostro at Queen’s University Belfast, UK, but the proposed set-up is specific and engineered in a way that won’t apply to many real quantum systems.

It is also important to understand that it doesn’t break the second law of thermodynamics, says Paternostro, because you have to spend energy to reduce the disorder of the system. “When I get into my son’s room, it’s a mess: balls are here and there, clothes are distributed across the room. If I make work, cleaning it up and ordering stuff, that will reduce the amount of disorder in that room, but I had to spend some energy,” he says. “That’s exactly what they are showing with their external control mechanism.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a novel scientific breakthrough in quantum physics that could enable new methods to harvest energy by reversing the arrow of time in quantum systems. This has potential broad implications for future quantum batteries and miniature quantum engines, representing a meaningful technological advancement with possible large-scale benefits.
articles.personality_title: Physicists find a way to reverse time in quantum systems to harvest energy
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Usually, time moves forward, and everything happens in one direction. This is called the arrow of time. In the tiny world of quantum physics, things can sometimes go both forwards and backwards, but usually, we see time moving forward because of how we measure these systems.

**What happened** – Scientists led by Luis Pedro García-Pintos found a way to make it look like time is moving backwards in certain quantum systems. They do this by carefully undoing the effects of measurements on these systems. For example, by using microwave pulses on a qubit (the basic part of a quantum computer), they can reverse how time seems to flow.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because when we measure a quantum system, energy gets added to it. By reversing time’s arrow, the researchers can redirect this energy and potentially use it to power tiny devices like quantum batteries or small quantum engines. This could lead to new ways to collect and use energy in the future.

**What's next step** – The scientists will likely work on testing this method in more quantum systems and improving how we control these time reversals. If successful, this could help develop real quantum batteries and other energy-saving technologies.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Researchers have found a way to reverse time in quantum systems, opening new possibilities to capture energy for future quantum devices.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des physiciens trouvent comment inverser le temps dans les systèmes quantiques pour récolter de l’énergie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Habituellement, le temps avance dans une seule direction, ce qu’on appelle la flèche du temps. Dans le monde très petit de la physique quantique, les choses peuvent parfois aller dans les deux sens, mais on observe généralement le temps qui avance à cause de la façon dont on mesure ces systèmes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques dirigés par Luis Pedro García-Pintos ont trouvé un moyen de faire paraître le temps comme s’il reculait dans certains systèmes quantiques. Ils font cela en annulant soigneusement les effets des mesures sur ces systèmes. Par exemple, en utilisant des impulsions micro-ondes sur un qubit (la partie de base d’un ordinateur quantique), ils peuvent inverser le sens du temps.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car, lorsqu’on mesure un système quantique, de l’énergie y est ajoutée. En inversant la flèche du temps, les chercheurs peuvent rediriger cette énergie et potentiellement l’utiliser pour alimenter de petits appareils comme des batteries quantiques ou de petits moteurs quantiques. Cela pourrait ouvrir de nouvelles façons de collecter et d’utiliser l’énergie à l’avenir.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont probablement tester cette méthode sur plus de systèmes quantiques et améliorer le contrôle de ces inversions temporelles. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait aider à développer de vraies batteries quantiques et d’autres technologies économes en énergie.

**Phrase clé** – Des chercheurs ont trouvé comment inverser le temps dans les systèmes quantiques, ouvrant de nouvelles possibilités pour capturer de l’énergie pour les futurs appareils quantiques.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos logran invertir el tiempo en sistemas cuánticos para aprovechar energía
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Normalmente, el tiempo avanza en una sola dirección, lo que llamamos la flecha del tiempo. En el mundo muy pequeño de la física cuántica, las cosas a veces pueden ir en ambos sentidos, pero generalmente vemos que el tiempo avanza debido a cómo medimos estos sistemas.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos liderados por Luis Pedro García-Pintos encontraron una forma de hacer que parezca que el tiempo va hacia atrás en ciertos sistemas cuánticos. Lo hacen deshaciendo cuidadosamente los efectos de las mediciones en estos sistemas. Por ejemplo, usando pulsos de microondas en un qubit (la parte básica de una computadora cuántica), pueden invertir la dirección del tiempo.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque cuando medimos un sistema cuántico, se añade energía. Al invertir la flecha del tiempo, los investigadores pueden redirigir esta energía y usarla para alimentar dispositivos pequeños como baterías cuánticas o pequeños motores cuánticos. Esto podría crear nuevas formas de recolectar y usar energía en el futuro.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos probablemente probarán este método en más sistemas cuánticos y mejorarán el control de estas inversiones del tiempo. Si tiene éxito, podría ayudar a desarrollar baterías cuánticas reales y otras tecnologías que ahorren energía.

**Resumen en una frase** – Los investigadores encontraron cómo invertir el tiempo en sistemas cuánticos, abriendo nuevas posibilidades para capturar energía para futuros dispositivos cuánticos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed painting of a glowing, stylized quantum battery shaped like a softly pulsing crystal, surrounded by swirling, translucent waves of light flowing both forwards and backwards in time, with delicate, abstract representations of qubits as shimmering, spinning orbs emitting gentle microwave pulses, all rendered in warm, natural hues of amber, soft blues, and muted golds.
